dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 17 responding for unconscious/fainting/near-fainting. Location is --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Good evening, I'm Taylor, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My husband was found in the chair with slurred speech and a flaccid right arm. He seemed very weak and was nauseated.
Ma'am, I'm going to check his responsiveness by asking him a few questions. Sir, what day is it today? Can you tell me your name?
He tried to speak, but it's very slurred. I can only understand him when he repeats himself.
Eyes are open spontaneously. Pupils measure about 3mm and are equal and reactive, though a bit sluggish.
I am assessing whether he is alert and oriented now. He is not A&O due to slurred speech, but he did attempt to follow commands.
He attempted to identify things, like a watch, but could not answer clearly due to his slurred speech. I can't determine neglect.
He appears slightly lethargic, but does respond to simple commands.
No active bleeding is observed-no obvious signs of hemorrhage or trauma on initial inspection.
Respiratory rate is 16 breaths per minute. Breathing does not appear labored.
I will take his blood pressure and pulse now.
His pulse is currently 66 - slow but regular.
Respiratory rate is 16 breaths per minute. Breathing does not appear labored.
Blood pressure is 125 over 76. That's within the normal range, but on the lower side of normal.
Ma'am, could you tell me about the signs and symptoms he has been experiencing today?
He was fine this afternoon until 5:45, when he called me in. I found him on the ground. He had a flaccid right arm and facial droop.
He had a CVA in 1992. His right arm has been flaccid since then. He has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and prostate cancer as past conditions.
He had a small meal at 2 o'clock today and has been drinking water throughout the day. He hasn't eaten a full meal since then.
Sir, may I check your pupils and head? We will be using a light and touch for the cranial area.
Pupils are 3mm, equal and reactive, though a bit sluggish. No signs of head trauma.
His skin is extremely diaphoretic - soaked, warm, and a bit pale. No rash, lacerations, or signs of jaundice.
Sir, I'm going to assess your motor functions. Would you try to squeeze my finger if you can?
His right arm is totally flaccid. The left arm has some movement when he tries.
Sir, do you feel any pain at the moment? If so, can you tell me what part of your body it is?
He doesn't seem to be in any pain at all. The main issues are weakness and speech difficulties, but no pain reported.
I cannot assess for neglect due to slurred speech - he was unable to follow more complex commands or respond coherently.
He did not seem to have receptive aphasia as he attempted to follow my commands, though the response was very slurred.
Airway is patent. He is not in distress and no interventions are needed at this time.
I'll check his temperature now using a tympanic thermometer. Tympanic temperature reads 96.4 degrees Fahrenheit.
I'm going to check his blood glucose. You may feel a small prick in a moment. Blood glucose is 140.
His SpO₂ level is currently 100 percent.
I'll be doing a 4-lead ECG now as part of our monitoring.
Your heart rhythm shows sinus bradycardia. It was difficult to get a clear ECG even with Benzoin and chest wipes.
He is on multiple medications, including amlodipine, hydrochlorothiazide / lisinopril, atorvastatin, Omeprazole, senna lax, dorzolamide, and multivitamin.
I'm going to begin an IV line now in his left AC vein as a saline lock.
Obtained 12 lead ECG showing sinus bradycardia.
12-lead ECG is back showing consistent sinus bradycardia.
Based on the field impression of stroke, we are activating the medical stroke/TIA protocol.
I believe VCU Medical Center is the most appropriate due to its stroke capabilities.
I trust your decision. I agree with VCU.
We need to move him now with minimal disruption. I'll use a stair chair to maneuver him from the chair to the stretcher.
We are securing the patient now. He's stable enough for rapid transport.
The patient is male, 86 years old, and his name is Mike. Weight is 68 kg.
Arrived at the hospital with some delay due to patient turnover and documentation.
I'm retaking his pulse. Pulse is now 55 - slower.
His blood pressure has decreased to 108 over 56, and SpO₂ is 99.
I'm going to check blood glucose again. It remains at normal levels.
I'll repeat the ECG in the 12-lead format. Pulse remains at 55 - stable rhythm with no change.
Blood pressure now is 127 over 68. Pulse is 61 - slightly higher.
He shows signs of a possible stroke and we maintain the stroke alert.
I am providing a pre-alert to the receiving hospital at VCU. This is a stroke alert.
Arriving now-let's confirm transport status again before we unload.
We have confirmed the stroke alert status. I'll begin the verbal report with the receiving team.
Our field impression is stroke (CVA infarction) with a history of a prior CVA, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and prostate cancer.
Currently taking atorvastatin, amlodipine, hydrochlorothiazide/lisinopril, and multivitamin at home with no known allergies.
Blood pressure was 108/56, pulse 55, SpO₂ 99, and blood glucose of 140. The EKG showed sinus bradycardia.
Right arm total flaccidity is chronic. Left arm has weak but functional movement. Patient is very diaphoretic but no active bleeding is seen.
Slurred speech is consistent with CVA presentation and he demonstrated attempts to follow commands which implies possible intact cognition and comprehension.
Patient was safely transferred from the stair chair to stretcher.
Patient is secured to the stretcher and ready for transport.
En route to VCU ED with lights and sirens.
VCU ED is active and awaiting us as stroke alert.
I've provided pre-arrival notice about this stroke alert, time-sensitive case.
Airway remains open and patent.
Breathing and oxygen saturation stable.
Pulse regular and within acceptable range.
No major deterioration in neuromuscular function or level of consciousness.
No response to complex questions, but the patient still attempts to follow simple commands.
Transport status is still stable.
Documented neuro assessment, no JVD, no trauma, and no active bleeding. Determined A&O as negative due to established cognitive baseline from spouse.
I'm ready to give a verbal report to the receiving team at VCU.
Mike is an 86-year-old male with a history of stroke, hypertension, and prostate cancer. Priority stroke alert.
Time of patient arrival will be approximately 5-10 minutes depending on traffic.
IV line is in place and patency has been confirmed.
No changes in condition noted during transport. Patient condition is stable but critical.
Patient will be transferred using sheet drag for smooth transition onto bus.
Ready to hand the patient off to the receiving team.
Same findings from the right arm-no strength detected. Left arm shows some volitional movement.
Diaphoresis persists and no new rash or bumps detected.
Head is still intact, no trauma noted.
Sliding him onto the hospital stretcher should be done with two attendants due to weak motor activity.
Hospital staff shall assist with safe transfer to hospital bed.
Receiving hospital is responsible for final securing.
Pupils maintained at 3mm, equal and reactive.
Examiner notes repeat motor function in the left arm and absence in the right arm.
Slurred speech consistent with chronic condition but with no new signs of aphasia or neglect.
Patient handover has occurred.
GCS score is 12 (Eye 4, Verbal 2, Motor 6) due to incoherent speech but correct head and hand placement upon command.
Initial suspicion was stroke due to sudden onset of neurological deficit and history of prior CVA.
Final transfer of care complete.
En route time was four minutes with lights and sirens engaged.
We departed the residence at 17:58 and are on ETA at VCU as the receiving hospital.
Slight delays at the receiving facility due to patient turnover and documentation load.
Arrived at VCU at 18:24. Pre-arrival alerts received and acknowledged.
I have completed and documented the pre-alert communication with VCU.
Patient's blood pressure and other vitals remain within acceptable range, and no new neurological deficits are evident.
Handoff is fully complete with verbal report and documentation.
Bed transfer was smooth using sheet drag as planned.
Patient is not of childbearing age-documented NA.
Patient had a light meal two hours prior with no recent ingestion.
12-lead ECG and all monitoring devices are handed off successfully.
Patient is now under direct care of hospital staff.
Stable, constant vitals.
All vital signs, interventions, and assessments have been reviewed and corroborated during handover.
Urgent transport was selected as the patient is a stroke alert with possible non-STEMI.
Total crew was two. Additional hospital staff assisted at the hospital.
All transport operations concluded.
Ambulance is prepared for return.
No equipment malfunction and emergency report is finalized.
Patient is in good condition per hospital assessment, with no acute deteriorations reported.
Completing full documentation now.
'AXO' status undetermined due to unclear verbal response and established new speech baseline.
No new signs of aphasia-receptive abilities seem to remain.
All checkmarks resulted in a solid plan, no loop errors detected.
Safe return approved.
Awaiting mission assignment.
Full transport and care cycle has come to a close.
No unreported symptoms or findings during care and transport.
Unit is prepared for the next call.
